Useful in review workflows where external parties need to mark documents as draft or pending before final signatures are placed.

Controls whether signature fields belonging to other signers are visible during multi-party external signing. Default: true. When set to false, each external signer only sees their own designated signature fields, and the property pdf.signature.aes.includepersonaldata is ignored for extern signatures, meaning no personal data is included in other signers' visible fields. Can be configured per user via settings.extern.multi.showOtherSignaturefields.available. Hiding other signers' fields improves privacy in sensitive multi-party scenarios but reduces the signer's context about where other parties will sign on the document.

Specifies the default signing order when extern.multi.available is enabled for multi-party external signing. Default: false (no enforced order, parallel signing). When set to true, signers must complete their signatures in a defined sequential order. Note: if the QES (Qualified Electronic Signature) button is favored, online editing forces sequential signing order regardless of this setting's value. This property sets the default behavior which can potentially be overridden per session. Sequential signing is important for workflows where later signers need to see earlier signatures before signing themselves.

Controls whether the 'Add form fields' mode is available in the PDF editor. When set to true, the mode is activated automatically, allowing users to add text fields, checkboxes, signature fields, and other form elements directly onto the PDF document. Default: true. When set to false, users cannot add new form fields and can only interact with pre-existing fields in the document template. This is primarily a consultant/creator feature for building signing templates. The form fields added through this mode become interactive elements that signers can fill in during the signing process.

Controls whether the 'No release' overlay action is available for the process creator/consultant. Default: false. When enabled, the consultant can stamp an overlay image onto the center of a document page, typically used to mark documents as 'not released', 'draft', or 'void'. This provides a visual safeguard against premature distribution of unsigned documents. The overlay image is rendered directly onto the PDF content. For external users, a separate property extern.page.overlay.available controls the same functionality. The overlay is a non-removable stamp once applied to the document.

Controls whether the user can change the order of manually added documents within a signing session. Default: false. When enabled, documents that were manually uploaded or added to the session can be reordered via drag-and-drop or order controls in the document list. This does not affect the order of template-based documents that are part of the original process definition. Useful in scenarios where multiple supplementary documents are added during a session and their presentation order matters for the signing workflow or final document package assembly.

Controls whether form field editing is allowed in the document. This is a document-level property set directly on individual documents rather than as a global guiProperties setting. When enabled, users can interact with and modify form fields (text inputs, checkboxes, dropdowns) embedded in the PDF document. When disabled, all form fields are rendered as read-only, preventing any modifications. This provides granular per-document control over form interactivity, useful when some documents in a session should be editable while others should be locked down for signature-only interaction.

Controls whether document upload by the user is enabled in the session. Default: true. Alias: add.doc.available. When enabled, users can upload additional PDF documents to the signing session from their local device. This can be overwritten per session via the Configure Session API. Useful for scenarios where customers need to attach supporting documents such as identification, proof of address, or supplementary forms. The uploaded documents become part of the session's document set and can be signed alongside the original documents. Disabling this restricts the session to only pre-configured template documents.

Controls whether a browser warning dialog is shown when the user attempts to navigate away from the signing page (triggered by the beforeunload event). Default: true. When enabled, the browser displays a confirmation dialog asking the user if they really want to leave the page, preventing accidental loss of unsigned documents or in-progress signing sessions. This is a standard browser behavior that protects against accidental navigation, page refresh, or tab/window closure. Disabling this allows users to leave without warning, which may be appropriate for embedded iframe scenarios where navigation is controlled by the parent application.

Controls whether a hint popup is displayed after the last signature has been placed, prompting the user to save or complete the process. Default: false, Recommended: true. When enabled, once all signature fields have been signed, a notification appears guiding the user toward the completion step. This improves the user experience by clearly indicating that all required signatures are done. Related property gui.hint.allsigned.skipSaveDialog can be used to skip the save dialog and go straight to the final completion dialog. This visual feedback is particularly helpful for documents with many signature fields.

Enables the process manager progress counter in the UI, showing the number of signed signatures relative to the total required, along with document and transaction display information. Default: false, Recommended: true. When enabled, users see a real-time progress indicator that updates as signatures are placed. In case of clustering (multi-server deployments), the progress display may not show. Related property gui.progress.optional adds a separate line showing optional signature fields. This feature significantly improves user orientation in complex documents with many signature fields by providing clear visual feedback on completion status.

Controls whether Quick-Tips are displayed in the UI — small contextual help popups that appear at relevant interaction points to guide users. Default: false, Recommended: true. Quick-Tips provide inline assistance without requiring users to open a separate help section. Related properties include quicktips.tutorial.enabled, which controls a more comprehensive tutorial variant that walks new users through the entire interface, and quicktips.reset.days, which controls the automatic reset interval after which tips reappear. Quick-Tips are dismissed individually and their state is remembered per user session.

Controls whether dualscreen mode is available in the editor interface. Default: false. WARNING: This property is unfinished and should NOT be used in production environments. When enabled, it activates an experimental dualscreen layout intended for setups where two displays are used simultaneously — for example, one screen for the consultant and another facing the customer. The feature is incomplete and may exhibit unexpected behavior, rendering issues, or missing functionality. Do not enable this in customer-facing deployments. It remains in the configuration schema for internal development and testing purposes only.

Controls whether the signer must input their real name after placing a signature. Default: false, Recommended: true. When enabled, a dialog prompts the signer to enter their full legal name immediately after making a signature, which is then associated with that signature in the document metadata. This strengthens the legal validity of the signature by linking it to a declared identity. Often used together with realName.input.skippable, which controls whether the user can skip this input. The captured name is stored in the signature's metadata and can appear in the PDF signature field's reason or annotation.

Controls whether the signer can skip the name input dialog that appears after placing a signature. Default: false, Recommended: true. Only relevant when realName.input.required is enabled. When skippable is true, the name input dialog shows a 'Skip' button allowing the signer to proceed without entering their name. When false, the signer must provide a name before continuing. Setting both required and skippable to true creates a soft requirement — the system asks for the name but doesn't block the workflow if the user chooses not to provide it, balancing identity capture with user convenience.

Controls whether the signer must input their location (city/place) after placing a signature. Default: false, Recommended: true. When enabled, a dialog prompts the signer to enter their signing location, which is recorded in the signature metadata and can be rendered alongside the timestamp on the signature. This is important for legal contexts where the place of signing has contractual significance. Works with realLocation.input.skippable to control whether the input can be bypassed. The location data may also be used in conjunction with settings.realLocation.available for pre-configured location values in the settings modal.

Controls whether the signer can skip the location input dialog that appears after placing a signature. Default: false, Recommended: true. Only relevant when realLocation.input.required is enabled. When set to true, a 'Skip' button appears in the location input dialog, allowing users to proceed without providing their signing location. When false, location entry is mandatory and blocks further interaction until provided. Similar to realName.input.skippable, setting both required and skippable to true creates a recommended-but-optional input flow that maximizes data capture while maintaining a smooth signing experience.

Controls whether the gallery user can change their name in the settings modal dialog. Default: false. When enabled, a name field appears in the settings modal allowing the user to update their display name. This name may be used in signature metadata, email communications, and the session UI. The settings modal must be enabled (settings.available=true) for this option to be accessible. This is particularly relevant for shared-device scenarios where different users sign on the same device and need to identify themselves, or for correcting auto-populated name information.

Controls whether the 'Location' setting is available in the settings modal dialog. Default: false. When enabled, users can pre-configure their signing location in the settings, which is then used as the default value when realLocation.input.required prompts for location input. Important: this setting requires settings.timestamp.available to also be enabled, otherwise the location value will not be printed on signatures even if configured. The location setting works in conjunction with the timestamp to produce a complete 'Signed at [location] on [date]' annotation on the placed signature in the PDF.

Controls whether the 'Timestamp' setting is available in the settings modal dialog. Default: false. When enabled, users can configure timestamp display preferences for their signatures. The timestamp records the date and time when the signature was placed. This setting must be enabled for settings.realLocation.available to function, as the location is printed together with the timestamp on the signature. The timestamp setting controls the visual rendering of date/time information on the placed signature in the PDF document. Related to pdf.signature.date.enabled, which controls the underlying timestamp data inclusion.

Controls whether the geographic location of tablets and mobile devices should NOT be queried via the browser's geolocation API. Default: false (meaning GPS is queried by default). When set to true, the application will not request GPS/location permissions from the browser, avoiding the location permission popup on mobile devices and tablets. This is useful in environments where location tracking is unwanted due to privacy regulations or where GPS queries cause unwanted browser permission dialogs. The location data, when collected, can be used for audit trail purposes and signature metadata enrichment.

Controls whether available personal data (name, email address, phone number) is included in the PDF 'Reason' field for AES (Advanced Electronic Signature) handwriting signatures. Default: true. When enabled, the signer's personal information is embedded directly into the signature metadata within the PDF, providing additional identity verification. For external multi-party signatures, the property extern.multi.showOtherSignaturefields is also relevant — if that property is false, personal data inclusion is suppressed for extern signatures regardless of this setting. This enhances signature traceability but may raise privacy concerns in certain jurisdictions.

Controls whether a datetime stamp and real location (if realLocation settings are enabled) are added to placed signatures in the PDF document. Default: true. When enabled, each signature includes a visual timestamp showing when it was placed, and optionally the signing location. This provides a chronological record directly visible on the signed document. The timestamp format follows the session's locale setting. This visual timestamp complements the cryptographic timestamp in the PDF signature metadata. Disabling this creates signatures without visible date/time information, though the metadata may still contain timing data depending on the signature level.

A root-level text property specifying the full name of the user who owns the signing session. This value is displayed in the UI alongside the session, appearing in session lists, headers, and identification areas. The name is also used in email communications and may be included in signature metadata depending on other property configurations. Unlike settingsNameAvailable which allows runtime editing, this property is set during session creation via the API and represents the authoritative identity of the session owner. Supports Unicode characters for international names.

The display name of the signing session, shown in the user interface, session lists, and email communications. This is a root-level session property set during session creation. It serves as the human-readable identifier for the session, distinguishing it from other sessions in the gallery/list view. The display name appears in the session header, notification emails to external signers, and administrative session overviews. It should be descriptive enough to identify the purpose of the signing session, such as 'Insurance Contract - John Doe' or 'NDA Agreement Q1 2025'. Supports Unicode characters and special characters.

The language code for the session's user interface, following the ISO 639-1 standard (e.g., 'de' for German, 'en' for English, 'fr' for French). This root-level session property determines the language used for all UI labels, buttons, messages, tooltips, and system-generated content within the signing session. It also affects date and number formatting. The locale applies to both the consultant/internal view and the external signing view. If not specified, the system falls back to the default locale configured on the inSign instance. Email templates may also be selected based on this locale.

The expiration timestamp for the signing session, specified in milliseconds since the Unix epoch (January 1, 1970 UTC). After this timestamp, the session becomes inaccessible and external signing links will no longer work. This root-level session property is set during session creation and provides automatic session lifecycle management. Expired sessions cannot be signed, completed, or modified. This is important for time-sensitive documents such as offers, quotes, or contracts with acceptance deadlines. If not set, the session uses the system default expiration configured on the inSign instance.

Specifies the default signature level for documents in the session. Supported values: SES (Simple Electronic Signature — text input based), AES (Advanced Electronic Signature — handwriting/drawing based), AESSMS (Advanced Electronic Signature with SMS verification — text input with mobile number verification for added identity assurance), and QES (Qualified Electronic Signature — highest legal standing, requires identity verification through a trust service provider). This root-level session property determines the signature method presented to signers. Different signature levels have different legal implications and user experience flows. Can be overridden at the document or field level.

The subject line of the email sent to external signers when they are invited to process a signing session. This root-level session property supports dynamic placeholders such as {name} for the signer's name, allowing personalized email subjects. The subject line appears in the recipient's email inbox and should clearly communicate the purpose of the email. Example: 'Signing request: {name} — Please review and sign your documents'. If not specified, the system uses a default translated subject based on the session locale. Keep subjects concise and descriptive for optimal email deliverability.

The body text of the email sent to external signers when invited to process a signing session. This root-level session property supports dynamic placeholders such as {name} for personalization. The email body should explain what the recipient needs to do, provide context about the documents to be signed, and any relevant instructions. The signing link is automatically appended or embedded by the system. Supports HTML formatting for rich-text emails. If not specified, the system uses a default translated email body based on the session locale. Should complement the externEmailBetreff subject line.

The email subject line for app-less (browser-based) signing invitation links sent to customers. This root-level session property is used when the signing process is configured for browser-based access without requiring the inSign mobile app. The subject should clearly indicate that the recipient can sign directly in their browser. Supports the same placeholder syntax as externEmailBetreff for dynamic personalization. If not specified, the system uses a default translated subject. This property is distinct from externEmailBetreff and allows different messaging for app-less versus standard external signing invitations.

The email body text for app-less (browser-based) signing invitation emails sent to customers. This root-level session property contains the message content explaining how to access and sign documents directly in the browser without installing the inSign app. Should include clear instructions for the recipient. Supports dynamic placeholders such as {name} for personalization and HTML formatting. The browser signing link is automatically included by the system. If not specified, a default translated email body is used. This allows tailored messaging emphasizing the convenience of browser-based signing without app installation requirements.

The email address used as the sender ('From' address) in all emails sent from this signing session, including external signing invitations, document delivery emails, and notification messages. This root-level session property allows customizing the apparent sender for branding purposes. The actual sending may be restricted by the mail.from server configuration, which can enforce allowed sender domains or addresses. If the specified address is not permitted by the server's mail.from rules, the system may fall back to the default sender address. Use a monitored mailbox if replies from recipients are expected.

Custom text for the exit/close button displayed in the burger menu and toolbar. This property allows overriding the default translated exit button label with custom text specific to the integration context. If left empty or not specified, the default localized message is shown based on the session's locale setting. Useful for providing context-specific button labels such as 'Return to Portal', 'Back to Dashboard', or 'Close and Return'. The custom text applies wherever the exit action label is displayed in the UI, including the burger menu dropdown and any toolbar exit button instances.

When enabled, the form editor is available for every editable document and always shows every available option including all signature field types (AES, SES, AES-SMS, QES), text fields, checkboxes, and image annotations. Without this flag, the form editor may only show a subset of options based on other individual addformfield.* properties. IMPORTANT: If allowFormEditing is set to false on the document level, the form editor will not be accessible regardless of this setting. Default: false. This is a convenience flag that effectively enables all form editor capabilities at once, useful for administrator or power-user scenarios where full document editing control is desired.

Controls whether signature fields requiring handwriting (AES — Advanced Electronic Signature) can be created via the form editor. AES is the default signature level in inSign, where users draw their signature on the device screen or via a paired smartphone/tablet. When enabled, the form editor toolbar includes the option to place AES signature fields on the document. These fields require the signer to provide a handwritten signature input. Default: true. This is the most common signature type and should remain enabled unless the workflow explicitly requires only text-based (SES) or qualified (QES) signatures.

Controls whether signature fields requiring only text input (SES — Simple Electronic Signature) can be created via the form editor. SES fields allow a user to sign a document by simply entering their name as text, without handwriting input. This is the simplest signature level and is suitable for low-risk documents or internal acknowledgements. When enabled, the form editor shows the option to place SES signature fields. Default depends on server configuration. SES signatures do not carry the same legal weight as AES or QES signatures in most jurisdictions.

Controls whether signature fields with mobile number verification (AES-SMS) can be created via the form editor. AES-SMS combines a handwritten signature with SMS-based two-factor authentication: after signing, the user receives an SMS code that must be entered to confirm the signature. This provides a higher level of identity assurance than standard AES. When enabled, the form editor includes the AES-SMS signature field option. The signer's mobile number must be provided either via the API (signConfig) or entered by the signer during the process.

Controls whether qualified electronic signature (QES) fields can be created via the form editor. QES is the highest signature level, legally equivalent to a handwritten signature under eIDAS regulation. Creating QES fields requires the user to have the INSIGN_USER_QES role assigned. The QES process typically involves identity verification and a certificate-based signing ceremony. When enabled, the form editor toolbar shows the QES signature field option. This should only be enabled for users and workflows that require the highest level of legal assurance and where the QES infrastructure is properly configured.

Controls whether the user can place image/photo annotations on documents via the form editor. When enabled, users can upload or capture images (such as photos of ID documents, stamps, or other visual elements) and place them as annotations on specific locations in the PDF. This is commonly used for attaching identity verification photos, company stamps, or other visual evidence to the signed document. The image annotations are embedded into the PDF during finalization. Default depends on server configuration.

Controls whether the consultant (session owner) can toggle form field editing capabilities on or off for external users on a per-session basis. When enabled, the session UI shows a toggle that lets the consultant decide whether the external signer should be able to add or edit form fields. This requires addformfield.available to be true as a prerequisite. Only allows editing of text fields and checkboxes for external users, not signature fields. Useful for workflows where some sessions need external form editing but others do not, giving the consultant control over this per interaction.

Controls whether the session owner (consultant/advisor) can use the inSign mobile app to provide their signature. When enabled, the session owner can pair their smartphone with the session via the inSign app, allowing them to draw their handwritten signature on the phone screen while the session runs on a desktop or tablet. The pairing can be permanent (default) or temporary per session (see beraterPairingTemporary). This is part of the signConfig object in the API request body. The app must be installed on the signer's device and registered with the inSign server.

Allows the session owner to sign without the inSign app by receiving a signing link via email. When enabled, the system sends an email containing a secure link that opens a browser-based signing interface on the recipient's device. This is an alternative to app-based pairing, useful when the signer does not have the inSign app installed. The email subject and body can be customized via applosMailBetreff and applosMailText properties in signConfig. The email is sent to the address specified in applosPerEmailEmpfaenger or the session owner's registered email.

Allows the session owner to sign without the inSign app by receiving a signing link via SMS. When enabled, the system sends an SMS containing a secure link that opens a browser-based signing interface on the recipient's mobile device. This is a convenient alternative when the signer has a mobile phone but no inSign app installed. The SMS text can be customized via applosSmsText in signConfig. The SMS is sent to the number specified in applosPerSmsEmpfaenger or the session owner's registered mobile number. Carrier charges may apply.

Allows the session owner to sign without the inSign app by scanning a QR code displayed on screen. When enabled, a QR code is shown in the signature popup that the signer can scan with their smartphone camera to open a browser-based signing interface. This is the fastest app-less pairing method as it requires no email or SMS delivery. Available since version 3.32. The QR code contains a secure, time-limited URL. This method works well in face-to-face scenarios where the signer is physically present with their smartphone.

Overrides the automatic touch device detection for the session owner. When enabled, the session owner MUST use a touch-capable device (tablet, smartphone) to sign directly on the screen, bypassing the normal pairing dialog that offers app, email, SMS, and QR code options. This forces direct touch-based signing without any intermediary pairing step. Useful for kiosk or tablet-only deployment scenarios where the signing device is always a touchscreen. If the device does not actually support touch input, this setting may cause usability issues.

Controls whether a hardware signature pad (U-Pad or similar USB-connected device) can be used by the session owner to provide their signature. When enabled, the system detects connected signature pad hardware and offers it as a signing option. Hardware signature pads provide higher-quality biometric signature capture with pressure sensitivity and precise pen tracking. This requires the appropriate signature pad drivers and browser plugin/extension to be installed on the workstation. Commonly used in bank branch, insurance office, and retail point-of-sale scenarios.

Determines whether the session owner's smartphone pairing is temporary (session-scoped) or permanent. Default: false (permanent pairing). When set to true, the phone pairing established during this session is automatically removed when the session ends. Permanent pairing (default) means the session owner's phone stays paired across multiple sessions, allowing faster subsequent signing. Temporary pairing is useful in shared workstation scenarios where different consultants use the same terminal, or for security-sensitive environments where pairing should not persist beyond a single interaction.

Timeout in seconds for automatically closing the signature popup dialog. Default: -1 (no automatic timeout). When set to a positive value, the signature popup will automatically close after the specified number of seconds if the user does not interact with it. This is useful for unattended or kiosk scenarios where a stale popup should not block the workflow. A value of 0 closes the popup immediately (not recommended). Available since version 3.41. The autoclose timer resets if the user interacts with the popup.

Controls whether the external signer (customer) can use the inSign mobile app to provide their signature. When enabled, external signers can pair their smartphone via the inSign app to draw their handwritten signature. This is part of the signConfig object. Unlike the session owner's app pairing, external signer pairings are typically temporary and scoped to the current session. The external signer receives pairing instructions as part of the external signing workflow. The inSign app must be installed on the customer's device for this option to work.

Allows the external signer (customer) to sign without the inSign app by receiving a signing link via email. The system sends an email with a secure browser-based signing link. The email subject and body can be customized via applosKundeMailBetreff and applosKundeMailText in signConfig. The recipient address is specified in applosKundePerEmailEmpfaenger. This is one of the most common methods for remote external signing, as it requires no app installation. The link is time-limited and single-use for security.

Allows the external signer (customer) to sign without the inSign app by receiving a signing link via SMS. The system sends an SMS with a secure link to a browser-based signing interface. The SMS text can be customized via applosKundeSmsText in signConfig. The recipient number is specified in applosKundePerSmsEmpfaenger. SMS delivery is fast and works on any mobile phone with a web browser. This is commonly used for remote customer signing scenarios. Carrier charges may apply and SMS delivery depends on the configured SMS gateway.

Allows the external signer (customer) to sign without the inSign app by scanning a QR code. When enabled in signConfig, a QR code is displayed that the customer can scan with their smartphone camera to open a browser-based signing page. Available since version 3.32. This is ideal for in-person scenarios where the customer is physically present — they simply scan the code from the consultant's screen. No email, SMS, or app installation required. The QR code URL is time-limited and single-use.

Controls whether a hardware signature pad (U-Pad) can be used by external signers during external processing. When enabled, external users can provide their signature via a connected hardware signature pad instead of on-screen drawing. This is relevant for scenarios where external processing happens at a physical location equipped with signature pad hardware, such as a customer visiting a branch office. Requires signature pad drivers and browser plugin on the workstation. Less common than the session-owner variant since external signing typically happens remotely.

Controls whether the external signer is allowed to change the phone number or email address that was pre-configured via the API through signConfig.applosKundePerEmailEmpfaenger or signConfig.applosKundePerSmsEmpfaenger. When enabled, the pairing dialog shows editable fields allowing the signer to modify the contact details. When disabled, the pre-configured values are locked and the signer must use the exact phone number or email address provided by the API caller. Disabling this is recommended for security-sensitive workflows where the signer's identity is verified through the provided contact channel.

Controls whether clicking 'next signature' or following the external user guidance arrow immediately opens the pairing/signing dialog instead of just scrolling to the signature field. When enabled, navigation to the next signature field automatically triggers the signing popup, reducing the number of clicks needed in the signing workflow. This streamlines the user experience especially for documents with many signature fields. When disabled (default), the user must click on the signature field after navigating to it. Works for both session owner and external signer navigation.
